title: Introduction post.
author: StockGPTpro
selftext:
Hey everyone,



Over the last year, I've been building something called StockGPT.



The idea is simple:



Every day, we rank all 500 stocks in the S&P 500 from strongest to weakest using a combination of over 50 quantitative signals, market data, and AI-driven analysis.



Most investing tools focus on helping you research one stock at a time.



We wanted to answer a different question:



"If you had to rank every stock in the S&P 500 today, what would that list look like?"



One of the things we've been tracking closely is how the highest-ranked stocks perform after being selected.



Our #1 ranked stock gained 50% this past month, while our #2 ranked stock gained 32%.



Obviously, no model is perfect and we're not claiming to predict the future. Some picks outperform, some underperform.



What I'm interested in discussing is:



\- How are people here using AI in investing today?

\- Do you prefer ranking systems or buy/sell signals?

\- What metrics would you want to see before trusting a model like this?

\- What are the biggest flaws you've seen in existing AI investing tools?



Happy to answer questions and share more about how we're approaching the problem.



Would genuinely love feedback from this community.

comments.author: Either_Door_5500
comments.body:
Ranking the entire S&P 500 daily across dozens of quantitative signals gets messy fast, especially when dealing with corporate actions. One of the biggest traps in historical fundamentals data is how you handle restatements and amendments. If your pipeline just pulls the latest numbers blindly, your backtests might suffer from lookahead bias because those numbers were not actually available to the market on the original filing date.

To make your quantitative signals more robust, it helps to build a proper data ingestion layer that separates raw, originally filed numbers from normalized, amended data. Tracking how a metric like revenue changes from a 10-Q to a later 10-K helps prevent your model from making decisions based on data that did not exist yet.

I have been working on an API in this space. It helps with structured and LLM friendly SEC data for devs, which might be useful for expanding the quantitative signals in your ranking engine without breaking your data pipeline.

Let me know if it sounds relevant.
